Narrative_Meaning: Queens signify an older female, matriarchy, scandals, social events, curiosity, love, wisdom, sinister manipulations and betrayal.

Yarn.Yarn_Description: The Scope card represents vision, examination, and focus. A scope can be used for assisted vision, it might be a telescope, a microscope, a periscope, reading glasses or a magnifying lens. It is a card that teaches us to focus on details, and to closely examine the facts or the scene.

Significator.Plot: The Scope Plot is a plot about investigation. Investigations can vary in type from genre and era, scouting parties, religious inquisitions, criminal investigations, scientific discoveries and expeditions are all with in the scope of The Scope.
Significator.Side: A side marked by The Scope are watching, observing, often making notes. They may be the media, a journalist, or herald, or an academic, a Chronicler, or historian, or they could be authorities, undercover police, agents and spies, or even aliens or Nephilim. The real question is are they watching everyone the same, or do they have a focus to their investigation?
Significator.Character:
The Scope Significator indicates a Character that revels in watching and investigating.

They Gain Yin when they make themselves comfortable, settle down, or adjust their position.

They Gain Yang when they discuss events they have witnessed, take notes, or record information.

They Gain Chi when they watch or observe an event, show, or process from beginning to end, focus on details or draw conclusions.

They gain +1 Success Levels to all actions to do with sensing, recording, or understanding information.
Significator.Scene: The Scope Scene is usually a Revelation, often where something is observed and revealed to the Characters (in extreme cases the villain will be monologue the Plot during a Scope Scene). Sometimes the Scope can be a Test of Senses or an Observation Ordeal (like a Stake-Out). Yarn-Tellers can play the card to pass any sensory Ordeal, or to gain a Revelation. They can also create Observation Ordeals for the target if they wish.

Yarn.Aspect_Description: The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.
Yarn.Hook_Description: The Hook hints at the Story with clues, background details, and no direct exposition of any sort. The Hook may even modify the Revelations of the Frame to make them into more specific details and less broad exposition. These Hooks may require setting multiple times during other stories before they finally catch the PC.
Yarn.Fray_Description: Watch Frays are Ordeals where PCs have to watch, observe and perceive something. It could be that they are watching their favourite TV show, or a film, the Ordeal will be the Psychological effects of the movie and the story, or perhaps the PCs are on a stake out, watching the enemy base, then again military watches are something else again. Who knows what might attack the party during the night.
Yarn.Snag_Description: The PCs are being watched, observed by someone, and it could complicate the Fray, for example speak-easy's always had lookouts who would warn of the Police approaching. Perhaps the scrutiny follows the Fray, with cameras, lights and microphones being thrust in their face moments after the Fray, as they are asked how they let the bad guys get away, or how you justify so many dead and injured...

NPC.Description: The Spy may be a foreign agent, or could just be the curtain-twitcher down the street.

Yarn.Sweeping_Text: The PCs finds, sees, or experiences some Revelation, and gives them a clue (usually a Key Test to spot) to a pair of Gains.
Yarn.History_Description: The Scope indicates that the history of the current situation is detailed, which usually means complicated machinations and random chance have both conspired to make this happen.
Yarn.Situation_Description: The Scope indicates that the current situation is one of scrutiny, this may mean you are forced to scrutinise, examining details, researching and looking into something, or be under some one else's scrutiny.
Yarn.Solution_Description: The Scope indicates that the solution to the current situation will be revealed by analysis, sometimes even the act of analysis itself will actually be the solution.

Revelations.About_Description: The information revealed is about the "Bad Guy", generally this is a Monster Character (often of the Dominant side, although Pressed Bad Guys are common to Tragic Storylines).
Revelations.Info_Description: Information that answers a Why question, like why are they doing it, or why is it/they that way?
Revelations.Vector_Description: The information is written, usually as a letter or email. It may be a correspondence to the Character, or be something they found or intercepted.
Revelations.Alternate_Description: Perceptions represent the information that the Embodiment has about the Player/PCs. "I knew, that you knew. I also know what you planned to do about it."
Revelations.Detail_Description: Quick details are of the blink and you'll miss it variety. They can be missed at first, and sometimes that will mean they are missed forever. Perhaps there was a flash of the face behind the mask, just after the smoke bomb went off. If the Player is lucky there may be video footage, a scrying attempt, or Femite recordings that can be viewed later, in slow motion.

Obstacle.Description: The way ahead is watched, usually by motion sensitive alarms, security cameras, guards or perhaps scrying. Any unhidden motion into the area (and some hidden probably) will cause alarms to be raised and security to completely block this route.

Tarot.Normal: +2 Success Levels (+38 Score) to all Target's tests to do with perception and senses while atop pile.
Tarot.Crossed: +2 Success Levels (+38 Score) to all Target's tests to do with hiding, sneaking, etc. while atop pile.

Ordeal.Obstacles: 2 Obstacles in every Non-Court Stage, 3 in every Court Stage
Ordeal.Stage_Description: The route ahead is very dangerous, mists hide details, the moisture coated rocks are treacherous underfoot, you are high, over broken spires of mist-shrouded rock, clinging to rough foot and handholds that were carved long ago.
Ordeal.Motional_Description: You may not know who is chasing you, or what they may do, if they catch up to your Hippogriff...
Ordeal.Tide_of_Battle_Text: The Ebbing side is trapped by their location, or circumstances. They may have a clif behind them, or have backed into a dead-end canyon, but they can no longer retreat. Instead they will have to fight their way out. The Ebbing side add +2 Pips to any card played to Attack. The Flowing forces will have a +2 Pips to any Disengagement Actions they make.
Ordeal.Fight_Description:
Queens come in two main types, politically powerful women (who may have a flair for violence, dark magic, manipulation or poisoning) and matriarchs noted for their huge (and surprisingly often violent, monstrous, or cannibalistic) families. Queens are often Monsters
  Facet    Monster  
Gossamer ⚊ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Description: The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. Competition, perception, endurance and curiosity are positive aspects associated with the suit, but spades also represent destruction, betrayal, distance, might and death. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.

Tarot Meaning: Misfortune, Suffering, Loss, Mourning, Treachery, Betrayal, War, Lawsuit, Conflict.

Ordeal Text: Ordeal Types: Confrontation (e.g. Warfare, Lawsuit), Competition (Sports, Quizzes etc), Perception (Spot Hidden). Spades act as Trumps for the Following Action Types: Attacking, Balance, Climbing, Contagion, Desecration, Destruction, Feats of Strength, Lock-picking, Navigation, Perception (Search/Spot), Staying power, and Stoicism. Spades can be used for any type of attack.

Parry:Redirect-AttackThe damage from the original attack (less the Parried Card) is redirected by the Defender to the Attacker, their Descendants or their Allies.

Soak: Counter-AttackThe matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate.

Wiry — The Monster is immensely physically powerful, although often its form may not reveal it. It often demonstrates supernatural power as well as strength and toughness. A small, strong Goblin, a robot that looks like a little girl, a cybernetically enhanced rat, or even a killer doll (when not an Object Monster) are all examples of a Gossamer Monster. The Wiry Monster can add Gossamer Score cards to its Monster Pool whenever it is underestimated, cared for, or demonstrates its strength. A Wiry monster may add their current Monster Pool Size Score (Number Reduced) Success Levels to any action with their strength or toughness. Wiries must spend cards from their Monster Pool as though it was a Prepared Defence to ignore damage.
Inertia ⚋ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.

Spades can be used for any type of attack.

Soak: Counter-AttackThe matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate.

Unstoppable — The Monster is inexorable, it does not stop and cannot be killed by any normal means. They start play with a Monster Pool of Inertia Score cards. Unstoppable Monsters can add Inertia Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they cross a threshold, pass a barrier, or are forced to stop or turn around. Unstoppable Monsters can ignore a number of Wounds equal to the number of cards in their Monster Pool. Wounds stacked beyond the Monster Pool affect them as normal. Inexorable Monsters can also ignore all Hitches with a Boon less than their Monster Pool Size. Whenever an Unstoppable Monster is damaged beyond this limit, it must Discard the Highest Card in the Monster Pool, it may immediately also Discard any Wounds lower than discarded Monster Card, any remaining Wounds are then discarded on a one Monster Pool card per Wound basis. However, while discarding these wounds and cards the Monster must remain still (playing dead) until a Chi Extend Duration equal to the Number of Monster Pool cards Discarded (the initial card is instant, the next discard takes an action, then a second takes a few seconds, the third Wound takes an Ordeal Round, 25 Wounds would take a week). Examples of the Inertial Monster type include the Hungry Dead (Zombies) and most robotic assassins (especially ones from the future).
Key ⚊ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Description: The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. Hunter — The Monster is a hunter or tracker, it should favour harrying attacks and hit and run tactics. Typically such a Monster will vanish or flee if it is wounded, only to reappear when healed. The hunter may be fooled by false trails, but never for long. Hunters may add Key Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they spot a track, clue, or similar, that indicates the subject of their hunt. Hunters receive a bonus to their Score or Pips equal to the number of cards in the Monster Pool on any tracking, attacking or hunting actions if they also spend a card from the Monster Pool on the action. Hunters may convert Mortal and Carnage Wounds that they take into Crippling Wounds instead, at a cost of one card from their Monster Pool. In T13, the archetypal version of this Monster is a Bulmäs Pack member from the Wild Hunt, but any Werewolf or Animal Shifter would probably qualify, as would robot cowboys in theme parks that have gone wrong, and any fantasy world’s Elven ranger.
Miasma ⚋ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Description: The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.

Mutant — The monster is a mutant or chimerical creature made from parts of others. This monster type is the oldest perhaps, you’ll find examples throughout the ancient world, particularly in Greek myth. The Mutant will often be larger, and may be malformed or unsymmetrical, with odd numbers of limbs, eyes, horns, or even heads. The Chimera will blend multiple animals together to create a new creature that combines features of all the “parent” creatures, preserving the best of all species. Mutant Monsters start with Miasma Score Monster Pool. They may add Miasma Draw cards to the Pool every time they accomplish a specified goal, such as eating a human, consuming a soul, bathing in radiation, or just growing a year older, etc. The Monster Pool can be used as a “Mutation” Annex, which can be used to mimic the abilities of the Mutants “parent” creatures, such as a spider’s ability to climb, or an eagle’s ability to fly. The Mutation Annex has a Boon equal to the Value of the Number of Cards in the Monster Pool, and so draws cards equal to the Monster Pool Size Reduced and plays a number of cards equal to the Miasma Draw. The Mutation Annex is often used as a Size Annex for the Mutant. Where a 5 card Monster Pool would create a Boon 16 Annex, and could grant a Kaiju Mutant the size of a Skyscraper. 1950s B-movie monsters are often of this type, giant ants, lizards, spiders, wasps, and even rabbits have been used. Classically of course, Cyclopses, Centaurs, Minotaurs, Satyrs, Gryphons (or Griffins), Hippogriffs, Dragons, Sphinxes, Manticores and of course the Chimera itself are all examples. You can also find examples in superheroes, especially those created by radioactive arachnids, or those with an X-gene.
Trial ⚊ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Description: The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. Soak: Counter-AttackThe matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate.

Killer — The Killer Monster is a deadly Combat specialist of some sort. Killers add Trial Draw cards to their Monster Pool each time they kill. Killers automatically add +1 Success Level per Monster Pool Size Score (Number Reduced) to all attacks, and Monster Pool Size Score (Number Reduced) is automatically added to the Pips of any attack as Penetration. Killers must spend Monster Pool cards whenever they will make a Trick either with other cards being played by the Ordeal Pool, or other cards in the Round Pool or a Style Reserve. Killers can be drug-addled assassins, crazed shooters, or just steel-souled soldiers.
Yonder ⚋ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Description: The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. Competition, perception, endurance and curiosity are positive aspects associated with the suit, but spades also represent destruction, betrayal, distance, might and death. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.

Soak: Counter-AttackThe matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate.

Alien — The Monster is an alien, an outsider. It may just be a metaphor for alienation, or literally be an extra-(or ultra)-terrestrial. Such monsters are usually obvious and inhuman, not just to look at, but in the way they think and behave. They may have apparently supernatural powers and abilities, but often make do with advanced technologies (in the form of additional Descendants). They may add Yonder Draw cards to their Monster Pool when they are observed doing their alien / inhuman / weird actions. They may add Monster Pool Size Reduced Success Levels to any action to do with their alien abilities, technologies, motion or survival (including Dodge, Soak, etc). Alien Monsters must play cards from their Monster Pool to socialise with “non-aliens”. From little grey Eusaiveans, through pod people and pepper-pot shaped engines of hate, all the way to face-hugging space-wasps, there are plenty of aliens.
  Facet    Monster

Lea.Notes: These paths open and close naturally through the Lea from time to time, they are easily walked by all Bulmäs, but Vermis and All-Walkers may walk them in Swarm or Dragon configurations.

Stress.Description: Stress can make monsters of us all, but worse still Stress can make Monsters, not just mountains, out of molehills.
Stress.Rules: The Character must pay Chi equal to the Character’s current Stress as Yarn. This Chi is used to purchase a new Character that can be a Force-of-Nature Extra with Monster Facets (Gossamer, Inertia, Key, Miasma, Trial or Yonder). These "Monsters" may actually be physical issues such as natural disasters, or they may be aspects of the Characters own Psyche, given hallucinatory or supernatural form. Then again they can be the Character themselves, unaware in their Stress induced black-outs what they are doing. Other times the Character will project their problems on an actual Monster, tying their Arcs together. The only catharsis available to a Character here is to defeat, or at least survive the Monster, which will relieve up to 12 Stress.

Trauma.Description: Trauma can make a Character wary in the future, they become vigilant, or even hyper-vigilant, always watching the shadows around them, always aware of what is happening, they will awaken if a floorboard creaks, and know exactly which stair it was. Their mind is constantly scanning their environment for any threat, no matter how large or small, no matter how unlikely, they are almost impossible to surprise, although they may drop their guard slightly immediately after they have spotted and avoided a potential threat.
Trauma.Rules: The Trauma makes a Character vigilant, or even hyper-vigilant, this typically adds the Character’s current Stress as Pips to all Actions to do with watching, vigilance or paranoid defensive behaviours (such as never sitting with your back to a door, keeping a gun or knife under your pillow, or sleeping in the hotel bath after arranging the bed to appear occupied) but this does not count as Straining. The Character can Relieve 1 Stress by finding a potential threat in their environment (such as spotting a figure hiding in shadows, or noticing a loose bit of carpet, and not tripping on it.

Age.Description: An Age of Inspection is occurring. During this age a society performs an inspection, sometimes this is a retrospective, examining the past, but it is more often introspective, where a society looks within itself. The exact reasons for this can be due to an political, judicial or scientific inquiry that catches the public attention, an artistic, journalistic, political, or social movement, or due to a new broom at the top, sweeping away corruption and criminality. Depending upon what is found and how the society responds to this knowledge can profoundly effect whether this age will be perceived later in a positive or negative light.
Age.Rules: During an Age of Inspection some society wide inquiry or inspection takes place, often this becomes an inspection of the society itself. With similar Questions (defined often by the society's highest Facet) being asked throughout the society.
